title: Weaning lambs
subtitle: Separating lambs from ewes at the right time and without unnecessary stress.
excerpt: Weaning is the point at which lambs leave the ewe and both animals start an independent feeding regime. Done at the right stage and with the ewe's condition in mind, it is a calm procedure. Done too early or badly timed, it sets both animals back. This covers when to wean, the separation method, and what to expect in the days that follow.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef and Lamb, weaning and post-weaning management. DEFRA Welfare Code for Sheep (2023). SAC Consulting Scotland, small flock management notes.

content.text: Most UK lowland flocks wean lambs at 12 to 16 weeks. By this point the lamb is eating grass and supplementary creep feed and no longer depends on milk for its primary nutrition. The ewe has dropped her milk production naturally.
at this stage is straightforward; the main task is reading the
and managing the udder during the drying-off period.

Weaning before 12 weeks stresses the lambs without much benefit to the ewe; weaning after 16 weeks ties up grazing land and delays the ewe's recovery of body condition before tupping. Check that lambs are eating grass confidently and not following the ewe constantly for milk before committing to a wean date. A lamb at 14 weeks that is grazing steadily and ignoring the ewe's udder for most of the day is ready to wean.

Move the ewes to a different field, out of sight and ideally out of hearing range. Lambs call loudly for 24 to 48 hours; so do the ewes. This is normal and the noise reduces by day three if the animals cannot see one another. On a small holding where fields are adjacent, a solid hedge or fence prevents the ewes being driven back to the fence line by the sound of the lambs.

Move the ewes onto thin grazing for the first week: less protein in the diet reduces milk production faster. Check each ewe's udder daily for the first five days. An udder that is firm but not hot and does not respond to stripping out is drying off normally. An udder that is hot, hard, and painful to touch, or that produces watery milk with clots, is showing signs of
and needs a vet check. Untreated mastitis can cause permanent half-udder loss.

Weaned lambs should go onto good-quality grass, ideally a rested pasture that has not carried adult sheep for a month (to reduce worm burden). Worm the lambs two weeks after weaning: the stress of separation can trigger a worm rise. Weigh them at weaning and at six-week intervals. A lamb gaining less than 100 g per day after weaning on good grass may have a parasite burden that needs addressing.
